data.title: Singapore marine fuel sales slip to lowest in 20 months
data.description:
Singapore marine fuel sales fell to their lowest in 20 months in February, official data showed on Friday.

Volumes at the world's top bunker hub totalled 4.15 million metric tons, down 7.1% month on month and sliding 8.1% year on year, data from Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA) showed.

Weak demand has weighed on the market since the start of the year, dragging premiums down, trade sources said.

Vessel calls for bunkering fell by 14.1% from the prior month to 3,075, hitting two-year lows, data showed.

Sales of the mainstay 0.5% low-sulphur fuel oil (VLSFO) softened sharply in February, totalling 2.07 million tons and hitting multi-year lows.

Singapore delivered-basis bunker premiums for VLSFO logged year-to-date lows, sliding to single digits by late February after holding in low $10s in January, sources said.

High-sulphur marine fuel (MFO) volumes dipped 1.9% at 1.62 million tons, though edging higher year on year.

In contrast, total marine gasoil sales rose 21.2% month on month at 314,600 tons.

Sales of alternative fuels held stable to higher. Marine biofuel volumes rose 2.8% to 110,900 tons, while a delivery for B100 or 100% biofuel was recorded for the first time.

Meanwhile, liquefied natural gas (LNG) bunker sales more than quadrupled to 30,000 tons in February.

Container throughput at Singapore fell by 5.2% to 3.32 million twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) in February, their lowest level since last September.

data.description:
Soiltech ASA (OSE: STECH) Sandnes, Norway, 14 March 2025 On March 13, 2025, Røvde AS, a company associated with Erik Bjøndal-Røvde,VicePresident Operations and primary insider of Soiltech ASA, sold 1,900 shares atan average price of NOK 53 per share in Soiltech ASA. After this transaction,Røvde AS holds 9 597 shares in Soiltech ASA. See attached form for furtherdetails. For more information, contact: Tove Vestlie, CFO Telephone: (+47) 90 69 06 48 Jan Erik Tveteraas, CEO Telephone: (+47) 95 21 49 25 This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section5-12 the Norwegian Securities Trading Act.

data.title: RLX Technology 4Q EPS CNY0.094 Vs. EPS CNY0.161 >RLX
Pilgrim's Pride: Board Approved Special Cash Dividend of $6.30/Share >PPC
Options Market Now Betting on Weaker Euro Amid Tariff Threat — Market Talk
data.description: Investors have switched toward a preference for euro put options that bet on the currency's fall amid concerns over U.S. tariffs, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. Last week's risk reversals showed a skew toward euro call options that bet on its rise. However, that skew now favors puts. "Helping that switch in sentiment is probably the looming threat of [U.S. reciprocal] tariffs in early April," Turner says. These tariffs could see Europe's economy "hit hard." Any additional negative tariff news could weigh on the euro further, he says. The euro also faces a hit if Germany's Greens refuse to back Chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz's fiscal spending plans, he says. The euro falls 0.1% to $1.0848. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0936 GMT - Investors have switched toward a preference for euro put options that bet on the currency's fall amid concerns over U.S. tariffs, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. Last week's risk reversals showed a skew toward euro call options that bet on its rise. However, that skew now favors puts. "Helping that switch in sentiment is probably the looming threat of [U.S. reciprocal] tariffs in early April," Turner says. These tariffs could see Europe's economy "hit hard." Any additional negative tariff news could weigh on the euro further, he says. The euro also faces a hit if Germany's Greens refuse to back Chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz's fiscal spending plans, he says. The euro falls 0.1% to $1.0848. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0926 GMT - Sterling's fall in reaction to the U.K. economy's surprise contraction in January looks modest, Monex Europe analysts say in a note. The currency has "largely looked through" data that showed the economy shrank 0.1% on month in January. "From a U.K. perspective, we are inclined to see the recent news that the government is axing NHS England as more consequential." This marks a more radical shift from a government that has so far taken an incremental approach to fiscal policy and was something that was needed, they say. This change is positive for the economy and sterling, the analysts add. Sterling falls 0.2% to $1.2925 and the euro rises 0.2% to 0.8296 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0922 GMT - Weak performance in the U.K. manufacturing sector resulted in worse-than-expected GDP growth in January, Moneyfarm's Richard Flax says in a note. U.K. GDP contracted 0.1% in January from 0.4% growth in December. "The result is a reminder that the growth conundrum remains a challenging one, and the Labour government appears no closer to solving it," he says. The rising global uncertainty around trade policies complicates U.K.'s growth outlook, Flax says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0920 GMT - The U.K. is struggling to find the growth that the government says is a priority for boosting consumer confidence, according to AJ Bell analyst Danni Hewson. U.K. GDP unexpectedly contracted 0.1% in January, below expectations of slight growth. "As surprises go it's a pretty unwelcome one, but when you're talking about an economy bouncing along the bottom, the big picture doesn't really change that much," Hewson says in a note. Indeed, the main positive for the month of people spending more on food shopping could be seen as households choosing to batten down the hatches and stay home rather than spend money out because they are nervous about the road ahead, she notes. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0919 GMT - The U.K. Labour government's growth plans are going awry, Ebury market-strategy head Matthew Ryan says after Britain's GDP contracted at the start of the year. That unexpected decline came amid multifarious points of uncertainty, not least Washington's plans to hit Europe with tariffs, Ryan says. But domestic troubles are also afoot, he says, pointing to planned tax hikes weighing on business confidence and investment plans. "With Labour's growth plans seemingly in tatters, it will be back to the drawing board ahead of the Spring statement" later this month, which should see cuts to spending plans and perhaps new tax increase, Ryan writes in a note to investors. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0913 GMT - The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield edges higher as markets await next week's Federal Reserve policy meeting. "The Federal Reserve's rate decision next week is expected to play a critical role in the bond market," DHF Capital's Erik Boekel says in a note. A dovish stance would likely to push yields lower while a hawkish outlook could provide support for the dollar and yields, the chief commercial officer says. Money markets price in the next Fed rate cut for June, according to LSEG data. The 10-year Treasury yield rises 2 basis points to last trade at 4.297%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0855 GMT - A disappointing setback in the U.K. economy at the start of the year shouldn't derail hopes for continual if gradual expansion over the rest of 2025, analysts at Investec write in a note. GDP fell back by 0.1% in January, figures show Friday. But monthly data are volatile and other indicators, including business surveys, point to expansion in economic activity, Investec says. The financial-services company expects the U.K. economy to grow around 0.1% a month, and by 1.0% for the year as a whole. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0852 GMT - Bitcoin rises as risk sentiment recovers on optimism that the U.S. could avoid a government shutdown. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said he would support a Republican funding bill set for Friday afternoon, removing the threat of a government shutdown. It marks a reversal in his stance after he vowed that he and other Democrats would block the bill. Hopes that a U.S. government shutdown will be averted is boosting risk appetite, helping U.S. stock futures to rise, Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. IG futures data show the tech-heavy Nasdaq opening 0.9% higher. Bitcoin has a strong positive correlation to the U.S. index. The cryptocurrency is last up 2.6% at $82,467, according to LSEG.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0843 GMT - The U.K. can expect an upward trajectory this year as increased government spending filters through to the economy, ING economist James Smith tells clients in a note. Britain's GDP unexpectedly contracted in January as production fell back. But that should change as the year goes on and the U.K. Treasury spends more day-to-day, and even if the government decides to pare back its spending plans a little, Smith says. "Given much of that spending boost will land in wages, the impact on the wider economy should be discernable in the GDP numbers this year," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0822 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall after U.K. GDP data came in weaker than expected, raising the possibility of more interest-rate cuts from the Bank of England. In January, U.K. GDP contracted by 0.1% on month, weaker than the 0.1% growth consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ poll. "January's GDP data offers little to inspire confidence [at BOE]," Secure Trust Bank's CEO David McCreadie says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield declines 2 basis points to last trade at 4.675%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent downplayed the dollar's fall this year but this is unlikely to indicate much for U.S policy on the currency, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. Bessent said in an interview with CNBC Thursday that the dollar's deprecation is a natural adjustment after last year's appreciation. "We doubt that means much/anything for the U.S. Treasury's dollar policy and he's just acknowledging the noise/disturbance this 'transition' period is having on the U.S. economy." Ultimately the Trump administration would prefer a weaker dollar but a global trade war is positive for the currency, he says. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - The Polish zloty could strengthen further in the near-term on the Polish central bank's cautious stance on interest-rate cuts and the continued strong performance of Polish equities, Danske Bank analysts say in a note. In a press conference Thursday following the central bank's decision to leave rates unchanged at 5.75%, the bank's Governor Adam Glapinski said there was no basis at all to cut rates. With the central bank having revised its inflation and economic growth forecasts higher for the coming years, it "seems like rate cuts are off the table in 2025 at least," the analysts say. The euro falls 0.1% to 4.1839 zloty and Danske expects it to reach 4.15 in the near term. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Air Lease Executive Chairman Steven Udvar-Hazy to Retire
data.description:
Air Lease said late Thursday that founder and Executive Chairman Steven Udvar-Hazy will retire May 2.

Udvar-Hazy will continue to serve on the company's board as nonexecutive chairman through its 2026 annual stockholder meeting.

data.title: Prices edge lower on strong wind power, Ukraine ceasefire talks
data.description:
Dutch and British gas prices continued to fall on Friday morning amid expectations of high wind power output and on talk of a possible ceasefire to the conflict in Ukraine.

The Dutch front-month contract (TFMBJ5) > slipped by 0.29 euro to 41.79 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) by 0907 GMT, on ICE.

The Dutch March contract (TFMBMK5) was down by 0.27 euro at 41.66 euros/MWh.

The British front-month contract (NGLNMJ5) was down 0.43 pence at 102.56 p/therm on ICE.

Stronger wind speeds were forecast for early next week, with gas for power demand in Northwest Europe expected down 201 gigawatt hours a day (GWh/d) at 2,908 GWh/d for Monday, LSEG data showed.

Attention has also turned to the possibility of a ceasefire in Ukraine after Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Thursday that his country agreed with U.S. proposals, but that any ceasefire would have to deal with the root causes of the conflict and that many details needed to be sorted out.

Putin also mentioned possible energy cooperation between the U.S. and Russia and mentioned a gas pipeline for Europe.

"The market is hedged between Russian gas returning and flows staying subdued," analysts at Energy Aspects said.

"There is still significant European and Ukrainian opposition to the restart of pipeline flows, as well as legal and logistical hurdles," the analysts said.

The Trump administration said on Thursday that the wind-down of a license allowing energy transactions with Russian financial institutions expired this week, raising pressure on Putin to come to a peace agreement over Ukraine.

"This might be a bullish driver for energy commodities in general but should not affect the European gas markets directly," LSEG analyst Saku Jussila said in a daily market comment.

In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) edged down by 0.80 euro to 69.80 euros a metric ton.

data.title: Sweco selected as a partner for the Rail Baltica Project Alliance in Estonia
data.description:
SWECO

Sweco has been selected as a member of a project alliance responsible for designing and constructing section 1 of the Estonian section of Rail Baltica, an EU-funded project to build a new high-speed rail link connecting the Baltic States with the rest of Europe. The project is scheduled to run until 2030. The contract will be signed and details finalised upon completion of the procurement process and standstill period. The estimated total cost of Section 1 (Tallinn Ülemiste to Pärnu) of Rail Baltica is approximately EUR 394 million, and the order value for Sweco is yet to be defined.

Rail Baltica is a joint transportation project involving the Baltic countries, aimed at establishing a new greenfield railway connection from Tallinn through the Baltic states to Poland. In Estonia the project will involve the construction of a total of 213 kilometres of entirely new European gauge (1,435mm) railway track, with completion expected by 2030.

“I am delighted that Sweco has been selected to contribute to this important project, linking the Baltic nations to the rest of Europe. As a leading European consultancy in the field of transportation, with 6,000 experts in mobility and active travel, Sweco is involved in a range of client projects in the ongoing green transition of Europe’s transportation sector. Our cross-border team is fully committed to beginning work within the Alliance once the procurement process is completed,” says Åsa Bergman, President and CEO of Sweco.

The Rail Baltica project is financed through a combination of European Union funds, national contributions from the Baltic States, and private investments. The EU plays a significant role, providing a substantial portion of the funding, through its Connecting Europe Facility (CEF) and other regional development programmes.

Press photos:

  • Visual of Rail Baltica train concept, free use, please credit: Rail Baltica
  • Photo of Sweco's Åsa Bergman, free use, please credit: Tobias Regell 

​​​​​​​Rail Bailtica’s announcement: Two international consortia declared successful in Rail Baltica's main line construction procurement in Estonia | Rail Baltica

About the client

Rail Baltica is a major infrastructure project aimed at creating a high-speed rail connection between the Baltic States (Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania) and the rest of Europe. The project will link the Baltic region to the European railway network, connecting cities like Tallinn (Estonia), Riga (Latvia), and Kaunas (Lithuania) to Warsaw, Poland, and beyond to other European countries. Read more here.

About Sweco’s transportation experience 

Sweco is a leading European consultancy in the field of transportation, with 6,000 experts in mobility and active travel, electrification and fossil-free fuels, traffic safety, and climate adaptation of infrastructure. Sweco’s experts deliver technical expertise in the investigation and project planning of new tracks and railways, as well as the operation and maintenance of existing tracks and accompanying infrastructure, to clients in the railway sector throughout Europe.

For additional information, please contact:

Anna E Olsson, Head of Press, Sweco Group, +4670 557 33 26, anna.e.olsson@sweco.se 

Sweco plans and designs the sustainable communities and cities of the future. Together with our clients and the collective knowledge of our 22,000 architects, engineers and other experts, we co-create solutions to address urbanisation, capture the power of digitalisation, and make our societies more sustainable. Sweco is Europe’s leading engineering and architecture consultancy, with sales of approximately SEK 31 billion (EUR 2.7 billion) in 2024. The company is listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. www.swecogroup.com

https://news.cision.com/sweco/r/sweco-selected-as-a-partner-for-the-rail-baltica-project-alliance-in-estonia%2Cc4118957

Press release

Rail Baltica Train Concept credit Rail Baltica

Sweco's President and CEO Åsa Bergman credit Tobias Regell
